straight_beat.name: Bobby Lee Chamberlain
straight_beat.column:
The Royals lost again, but not without a fight. Saturday’s game against the White Sox was a microcosm of their season — a mix of flashes of brilliance and frustrating lapses that leave you wondering if they’re chasing shadows. Kansas City’s offense, which has been a reliable punchline all year, managed to scratch out five runs, but the bullpen’s inability to protect leads has become a narrative that’s hard to ignore. The White Sox didn’t just win; they did it with a sense of inevitability, like they were playing in the Midwest box and the Royals were just… trying.  

The pitching staff has been a rollercoaster. Mike Minor started strong, keeping the White Sox hitters off balance for the first three innings, but then the wheels fell off. By the fifth, the offense had clawed back to within a run, and suddenly, the Royals were in a game they should have been able to control. That’s the Midwest box — a place where you’re always one pitch away from a collapse. The bullpen, which has been a liability all season, couldn’t find the strike zone, and when they did, they couldn’t finish. It’s not just about the numbers; it’s about the

optimist.name: Dottie Verlander (no relation)
optimist.column:
The Royals lost yesterday, but let’s not let that sour the brisket. A three-game skid? Please. Witt’s cleats haven’t even warmed up. This lineup is a slow-burn brisket and by June you’ll be begging for seconds.  

Look, I get it. A 6-5 loss to the White Sox stings. The Royals’ record now sits at 7-9, two games behind the division lead. But here’s the thing: this team isn’t built to win every game. They’re built to grow, to grind, to grind some more. And if there’s one thing I’ve learned from catering BBQs in Overland Park, it’s that the best flavors come after the fire has done its work.  

Bobby Witt Jr. is the heart of this

pessimist.name: Cletus Marburger
pessimist.column:
The Royals lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that feels familiar, like a bad dream you can’t wake up from. Chicago’s 6-5 victory yesterday wasn’t just another loss—it was a mirror held up to a team that’s been circling the same problems for years. They’re 7-9 now, 2.0 games behind the Central leaders, and if you’re looking for a sign that this team is finally turning the corner, you’ll need to look harder. Because the Royals don’t just stumble; they trip over their own feet, again and again.  

The front office has a knack for making decisions that feel like they’re written in a language only David Glass can read. You get one or two good players, they get traded. You get a ring, they fire-sale the roster. It’s not just the numbers—it’s the rhythm.
